original_abstract:
Throughout the 20th century, many scholars cautioned against the use of static, ontological, and metaphysical interpretive frameworks, which originated in Hellenistic thought and continue to shape traditional images of God in Christian reflection. In the wake of modern spiritual and philosophical shifts—such as the rise of apatheism (indifference toward the divine) and anatheism (the return to the sacred after the "death" of God)—questions regarding the relevance of existing theological categories have become urgent for pastoral theology. 

This paper proposes a paradigm shift from "omni-categories," which emphasize imperialistic power and distance, to "pathos-categories," which prioritize divine empathy and vulnerability. By adopting a theopaschitic perspective, theologians can reframe static, Roman-Greco images of a detached God into a more dynamic and compassionate presence. Central to this shift is the introduction of a "sustainable God"—one whose constant, compassionate presence (*ta splanchna*) is understood through "infiniscience" and a pneumatology of "to-be-with." This framework seeks to address contemporary crises of human suffering, dignity, and justice by emphasizing a God who is intimately involved in the complexities and contradictions of the human experience.
humanized_abstract:
The threat and potential of old and new imaging models of God is heightened by the fact that, during the entire 20th century, many exponents of theologies have warned against the use of the static, ontological and metaphysical imaging models that come from Hellenistic concepts of God and still determine traditional conceptions of God in Christian theology. Above all, with the arrival of modern forms of apatheism (indifference to God) and anatheism (the experience of the return to the God whom we believe to have died) this is a highly urgent question for pastoral theology, which must today question whether it is still possible to use these traditional models in the world we live in, or if, on the contrary, it has to use new models to be able to communicate the word of God also to new generations.

Every day we are confronted with so-called omni-categories such as all-knowing, all-powerful and the like. These categories reduce divine attributes to imperialistic and distancing terms; terms that in many ways obstruct a truly personal encounter with the divine. Instead of using omni-categories, we suggest using pathos-categories. In other words, instead of relating to the divine as omnipotent and so on, we should relate to it as having feelings and being vulnerable. Based on the theopaschitic model of understanding God, we reinterpret the classical, and often impersonal and Roman-Greco, representations of God into a more personal and God-image in line with the early church fathers. In particular, we use the concept of a "sustainable God" (in particular God's attributes *ta splanchna* - God's bowels/compassion), expressed through the philosophical terms of "infiniscience" and a pneumatology of "to-be-with" in order to understand and respond to the various crises we encounter in the world of humanity including suffering, violations of human dignity and justice. These crises and problems arise because humans have to live through and experience the full range of human life in all its diversity and contradictions. Therefore, God cannot distance itself from this complexity and ambiguity of the human condition.
